data.title: Alibaba: Apple Intelligence Partnership Is A Plus
data.description:
BABA Stock: FQ4 Earnings, Qwen3 Models

I last covered Alibaba Group Holding Limited (BABA) (OTCPK:BABAF) on April 30, 2025. In an article titled "Alibaba FQ4 Preview: Options Are The Way To Play An Earnings Surprise", I provided a preview for its FQ4 earnings. In particular, I analyzed the use of options and rated BABA stock as a hold based on the following considerations:

I expect significant profit declines in BABA's FQ4 earnings due to the uncertainties from ongoing U.S.-China tariff disputes. Further, I believe the current implied volatilities underlying BABA’s options underestimate such uncertainties. As such, I recommend considering options for either BABA exposure or hedging purposes.

Since then, there have been some materials developments surrounding BABA stock. In the remainder of this article, I will concentrate on the top 2 new catalysts as I see: the business updates BABA provided in its FQ4 earnings report [ER] and also the latest updates of its Qwen3 AI models, which is considered an important step toward its partnership with Apple for Apple Intelligence’s launch in China. After evaluating these new developments, I keep seeing a neutral risk/return curve and reiterate my hold rating.

BABA Stock: The Positives

Let me start with the positive developments I see in its FQ4 ER (and also its full fiscal 2025). Ali Cloud is the highlight that grabbed many investors’ attention, and this segment grew its revenues by 18% YoY and adjusted EBITA by 69% YoY in the March quarter, as you can see from its earnings slide below. Notable, AI-related products grew at a triple-digit rate for another quarter.

Besides the cloud segment, the growth in other areas of also encouraging. The most notable metric is the robust CMR data (Customer Management Revenue). As seen, CMR accelerated to 12% YoY growth in FQ4. Digging into the ER further, I believe this is primarily due to Taobao take rate increase and higher adoption of Quanzhantui. CMR is a key indicator of the health and growth of the BABA’s e-commerce business, its bread and butter. Looking ahead, I expect continued improvement of monetization to further help CMR to maintain healthy growth in the years ahead for several reasons. For example, I expect the incremental investments in Taobao insta-shopping and also food delivery to help traffic and engagement.

BABA Stock And Apple Partnership

Shortly after the release of the ER, Alibaba's also released updates for its Qwen3 AI models. As a background for readers not familiar with this development, Alibaba announced earlier that it will partner with Apple (AAPL) to support iPhones' AI services offering in China. Under the context, the new updates in the Qwen3 models are widely interpreted (see an example below) as key steps towards the arrival of Apple AI in China.

Seeking Alpha News (June 17) : Alibaba's update of its Qwen3 open-source, artificial intelligence models for Apple's MLX architecture, likely signals the eventual deployment of Apple Intelligence features to mainland China, according to the South China Morning Post. With the update, the Qwen3 models can run on Apple's iPhone, iPad, MacBook and Mac.

I consider these developments and eventual offering of Apple intelligence in China as a key positive catalyst for BABA (and Apple too, but for different reasons for another time). To start, the partnership is a strong endorsement of Apple on BABA and can boost BABA’s brand image and recognition substantially in China. The partnership can also position Alibaba as a key player in the on-device AI space given Apple’s enormous installed base in China.

Thus, I have no doubt that these developments will help BABA’s EPS growth in the years to come. But there are some concerns of growth in my model as to be detailed in the risk section. In any rate, the expected growth seems to be already captured in the current valuation ratios. For the next fiscal year of 2026, Alibaba is projected to growth its EPS of $9.78, reflecting a year-over-year growth of 7.76%, followed by a more robust 13.83% growth in FY 2027 and by an 11.75% growth in FY 2028. The forward P/E ratio at the current stock price is around 11.32, thus the PEG (P/E growth) ratio is noticeably above the ideal threshold of 1x (about 1.5x) based on the FY1 growth projection and about 1x based on the growth projection in the next 3 years.

Other Risks And Final Thoughts

Now let me switch to the negatives that I see from the ER. First, the profits did suffer in the quarter and missed expectations on both lines. To wit, for the March quarter, Alibaba reported a normalized EPS of $1.74, missing estimates by $0.04. The GAAP EPS came in at $0.72, missing by an even larger margin of $0.41. Revenue missed expectations too (although only slightly by $515.13 million). For reasons detailed in my last article, I am not surprised to see these misses, and I won’t be surprised by continued profit disruptions in the near future at the U.S.-China trade negotiations evolves.

Margins are under some pressure too. As seen in the chart below, based on the March quarter’s financials, my estimate for its GAAP net margin is 12.5%. This is lower than both the TTM margin of 13.06% and also the 5-year average of 13.1% (see the next chart below). Looking ahead to the upcoming quarter, the consensus is optimistic about a large improvement in both the earnings and also its margins. The market expects BABA's GAAP EPS to reach $2.03 on the revenue of $35.93 billion, thus implying a margin expansion to 15.4% in the next quarter. I remain skeptical of such an expansion given the trade disruptions in the 1H of 2025 and the overall macroeconomic conditions in China.

To recap, two developments since my last writing motivated this reexamination of BABA stock: the business updates provided in its FQ4 ER and also the latest updates of its Qwen3 AI models. After analyzing these developments, my overall assessment is that the current parameters surrounding BABA still represent a neutral reward/risk profile. I certainly see multiple growth catalysts, including its cloud services, the robust CRM, and also the potential collaboration with Apple. However, these catalysts are properly factored into its current valuation by my model. Furthermore, I still have some lingering concerns about its margins. These considerations have led me to maintain my hold rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Broadleaf Partners Growth Equity Portfolio Q2 2025 Review
data.description:

Performance Commentary

This quarter might best be described as the “Big Beautiful Bounce”. Or the BBB. History has proven time and time again that markets do come back – but this was a historically quick market turnaround. We think that bodes well for the rest of the year – but that doesn’t mean investors should take off their seatbelts.

Fund Inception 8/18/05. Portfolio performance reflects Broadleaf’s Growth Equity Composite, described more fully under the caption “Performance Disclosures.” You are urged to read that information in its entirety in connection with any evaluation of Broadleaf’s performance statistics. All figures are shown net of actual fees. Any assumed fees have been calculated on a pro forma basis, reflecting the highest fee levels that Broadleaf would charge clients per our disclosures in Part II of our Form ADV.

Market Review & Outlook

What is the value of information?

In the digital age, data, or information, has been likened to digital gold. It’s everywhere around us, and for companies hoping to harness the power of AI, the first and most important step is getting their data aligned—a fancy way of saying “organized.” Palantir (PLTR), a leading AI and data company, calls this process ontology, which is essentially a giant org chart for how and where information flows.

If that still sounds abstract, think of it like this: many companies are like third-graders who just dropped their overstuffed binders in the hallway—papers everywhere, scrambling to get sorted before the next bell. In the current state, it is likely that the wrong papers are brought to the next class. We aren’t even sure if kids still learn using paper and pencil, but nevertheless, the world has become obsessed with organizing their messes of information and data.

But not all information is created equal.

Take it from me: our basement recently flooded during one of Ohio’s seemingly never-ending summer storms. I had to learn quickly what to do—cut the electricity, avoid standing water, and test the well afterwards (sure enough, the water came back positive for E. Coli - yuck). This might be second nature to others, but not to a first-time homeowner.

On the other hand, I also recently learned that Albert Belle was snubbed for the AL MVP in 1995—the year I was born. As a diehard Cleveland baseball fan, this may help you impress your friends, but is not especially useful unless you're a baseball historian in Cooperstown.

In this age of information overload, the real challenge is discernment. Or knowing what matters. One blog post I read made me laugh—an AI model, asked to run a beverage company, got confused by a customer’s novelty order for a tungsten cube. Rather than ignoring the outlier, the model placed a full purchase order for more tungsten cubes, bankrupting the business. It mistook noise for a signal—something most humans would instinctively recognize.

That’s the key: wisdom is learned experience. It’s knowing which signals matter, and which are just static. It remains to be seen if machines can develop this kind of judgment. They don’t feel the pressure of missing payroll or losing a customer, and thus, they don’t really learn from mistakes—at least not the way humans do.

So yes, AI may help us learn faster. But we believe businesses will only go as far as their employees will take them—especially when making high-stakes decisions. Most real-world problems live in the messy space between what you can learn from a textbook and what only comes with experience. And – maybe I am old school, but a smile and a handshake still goes a long way.

In terms of the economy – employment is definitely in a better balance. Inflation remains under control thus far, in spite of tariffs. Employees don’t quite have the upper hand that they have gotten used to over the last few years, and are being sent back to their offices in droves. At the same time, companies have avoided significant layoffs, as they remain uncertain about the future. Future hiring plans are on pause. Some have started to blame AI for the weakening, but we think it is too early to substantiate this. If you are a jobseeker – none of this talk of averages or big picture matters, it’s not easy to find a job, and we empathize with you.

To the recent grads out there: if you're feeling anxiety about the future, you’re not alone. You can’t change the job market that you come into. But more often than not, technology ends up being an enabler, and the younger generations are always best equipped to help organizations implement technological change. At the same time, in a digital world, soft skills and relationships matter more, not less—it doesn’t cost anything to be kind and have a good attitude.

Perhaps it is an interesting parallel that as fewer of us work in fields that require physical labor, many of us now have to schedule movement—gym memberships, workouts, and walks that our ancestors got by simply living – hunting, farming, and commuting. Perhaps the same will happen with scheduling tedious mental activity. Crosswords and Sudoku just may replace monthly close processes and manual data entry. While we might mourn routine for a while, at the same time, our descendants will equally likely view us as crazy.

All of this is to say – 2025 has been a roller coaster year for markets. While discernment has been important, Patience is proving once again to be the most valuable investing virtue. Volatility has always been an inconvenient attribute of public markets, and in the world of immediate information and potentially even 24/7 markets, we don’t think this changes. We just ask that tariff announcements don’t interrupt our Fourth of July barbeques – it is a market holiday for a reason!

As always, we’ll continue to try our best to sift through the noise, apply the wisdom we’ve gained from touching our fair share of hot stoves, and patiently put our best ideas to work. Of our three cycles of value—Economic, Innovation, and Credit—you’ll notice there is no mention of a fourth: Geopolitical. While we will continue to monitor things closely, our past learned experience (wisdom) has taught us that patience through geopolitical events is often the best solution. We sure wouldn’t mind a little luck too!

Thanks, as always, for your trust and support. If you feel that we have done a good job for you, we would love to help any family or friends who could benefit from our investment management services, or perhaps a financial planning introduction.

Wishing you and your family a very Happy Fourth of July Weekend.

About the Author

Pete is an Associate Portfolio Manager at Broadleaf, with a strong background in the financial services industry. He joined the firm in 2021, and has a proven track record of analyzing portfolio companies and identifying growth opportunities. Pete is a CPA and received his Chartered Financial Analyst (CFA®) designation in 2024. Pete lives in Hudson, Ohio with his wife Nicole.

Portfolio Characteristics

Portfolio Statistics

Investment Style

The Broadleaf Growth Equity Portfolio employs a concentrated growth style of investing, holding approximately 25-35 equity positions from a cross-section of economic sectors. Morningstar would classify us as a large cap growth manager, but we will invest in select small and midsize companies as unique opportunities avail themselves. Sector exposures are strongly influenced by our views on three determinants of investment value, which we define as the economic cycle, the innovation cycle, and the credit cycle. Individual securities are ultimately selected on the basis of their long-term growth potential, profitability, and intrinsic value as measured by their free cash flow generating characteristics. Innovative new ideas and themes are of particular interest.

Investment Objective

The portfolio’s goal is to provide equity like returns and to outperform the S&P 500 over a three to five-year time horizon or full market cycle, utilizing a growth oriented investment style. The portfolio is suitable for investors seeking an exposure to a concentrated investment style which may be more volatile than the market as a whole. Investors should consider it as a portion of their investment portfolio within the context of their overall asset allocation and related investment goals.

Performance Disclosures

Results reflect the actual performance of Broadleaf’s Growth Equity Composite. Performance data is shown net of advisory fees and trading costs. Broadleaf may charge different advisory fees to clients based on several factors, but primarily based on the size of a client’s account. Broadleaf’s basic fee schedule is available on its Form ADV, Part II. Results reflect the reinvestment of dividends and distributions, if any. Leverage has not been utilized. The U.S. Dollar is the currency used to express performance.

Broadleaf’s growth Equity Composite includes all fully discretionary accounts utilizing our growth equity style of investing with a minimum initial account size of $250,000. (From firm inception to 6/30/2009 our minimum account size for composite inclusion was $250,000 and from 6/30/2009 to 6/30/2013, the minimum had been $100,000. Historical results have not been updated retroactively to reflect changes in account minimums, but are reflected on a going forward basis.) To be included in the composite, an account must have been under management for at least one full quarter. If a significant cash flow in an underlying composite account during the quarter causes it to deviate from our intended growth style, we will remove the account for the period in which the significant cash event occurred. A significant cash flow is currently defined as 10% or more. Total firm assets at quarter end were $534 million. Prior to January 5th, 2006, the firm did not have any investment advisory clients. As a result, composite data prior to March 31st, 2006 only reflects the performance of Doug MacKay’s personal retirement account.

The S&P 500 Index has been used for comparative benchmark purposes because the goal of the stated strategy is to provide equity-like returns and to outperform the S&P 500 over a three to five-year time horizon or full market cycle. The S&P 500 is a broad-based index reflecting the performance of the equity market in general. The S&P 500 Index is based on total returns which includes dividends. We monitor the performance of our growth style of investing by comparing our results to those of other large cap growth peers. While we believe these are appropriate benchmarks to use for comparison purposes, it should be expected that the volatility of the Broadleaf Growth Equity Portfolio may be higher due to its concentrated nature.

Performance information since inception reflects actual performance of the composite over a period of greater than fifteen years. You are cautioned that information concerning comparative performance over this period of time may bear no relationship whatsoever to performance over other time periods. This information should not be regarded as in anyway representing the likely future performance of the portfolio in absolute terms or in comparison to the indices. Investment in securities, including mutual funds, involves risk of loss. Past performance is no guarantee of future returns.

Yield should not be used as an indication of the income that has or will be received. We do not consider yield as performance but instead as an attribute relating to the income production profile of the portfolio and its underlying assets. Yield is shown without the deduction of fees and expenses. The overall effect of fees is best represented through the difference between gross and net performance.

Broadleaf Partners, LLC is a registered investment advisor with the Securities and Exchange Commission. The firm maintains a complete list and description of composites, which is available upon request.

Performance information contained in this document including any reference to the purchase or sale of a security, or a strategy, is not intended to constitute personalized investment advice. Personalized investment advice is always dependent on individual factors, involves risk and is not a guarantee that any investment will produce favorable results.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Kohl's: Tariffs And Debt Remain Challenging
data.description:
Shares of Kohl’s (KSS) have been a poor performer over the past year. The company has struggled mightily to regain traction and suffers from a bloated store count. On top of this, it has struggled with excessive debt, forcing the company to slash its dividend while trying to execute a turnaround. I last covered shares of Kohl’s in March when I reiterated the stock as a “hold.” Since then, shares are essentially flat, lagging the market’s 10% gain. With updated financials and new trade developments, now is a good time to revisit KSS. I remain cautious.

Vietnam Tariffs Are An Incremental Headwind

On Wednesday, we received more news on the tariff front, which, while not unexpected, is an incremental negative for Kohl’s and the apparel sector in my view. President Trump announced the framework of a US-Vietnam trade deal. Under this deal, imports from Vietnam will be subject to a 20% tariff, above the 10% baseline tariff that has been in place during negotiations with all countries. This is similar to the deal rumored several weeks ago, which led me to downgrade shares of Gap (GAP).

Vietnam has become a critical trading partner with over $100 billion of exports to the US. During the first Trump Administration, we began to see companies more aggressively shift production out of China and into Vietnam to avoid tariffs. This strategy is now less effective given Vietnam’s 20% tariff rate. There had been a widely held view that aside from China, tariffs would largely be in the ~10% zone. In reality, we are likely to see an effective tariff rate higher than this and closer to 20%, given sectoral tariffs.

As such, I do think investors need to be prepared for higher-than-expected tariff pressures on companies, particularly in the apparel sector. Kohl’s will likely push suppliers to eat some of these costs, but on balance, they will likely pressure margins to a degree. Given lackluster demand, the ability to raise prices to pass on incremental costs without further eroding sales is questionable in my view.

Recent Results Show Cost Discipline

This incremental headwind comes after Kohl’s reported okay Q1 results in what was a mixed quarter for the sector. In the company’s first quarter reported on May 29th, Kohl’s lost $0.13, which was $0.09 better than feared. Revenue fell by 4% to $3.2 billion. Same-store sales were down 3.9%, but gross margins improved by 37bps to 39.9%. With 1,153 stores, Kohl’s is the largest department store in the country, and I continue to believe it is oversaturated, contributing to these ongoing same-store sales declines. Plus, over 1,100 stores now have a Sephora, so this tailwind is fully in results.

The ability to sustain margins with additional tariffs remains an open question in my view. We also have seen KSS implement more cost discipline. SG&A fell by 5.2% to $1.16 billion. At 36% of revenue, this was a 30bp improvement, though the improvement was partially due to the reclassification of credit expense from SG&A to other revenue. Now, alongside results, Kohl’s maintained guidance, even factoring in tariffs implemented at that time.

Given incremental tariffs, this may pose some risk to guidance. I do not expect additional Vietnam tariffs to dramatically alter the outlook, but they do present another downside risk. Moreover, a higher blended tariff rate is likely to pressure overall inflation and put some downward pressure on consumer spending. This comes as apparel spending has finally passed its post-COVID high. Spending is still well above its long-term trend, though there appears to have been a structural shift post-COVID. Still, I would expect apparel consumption to grow more slowly than overall consumer spending.

In the company’s first quarter, Kohl’s generated -$220 million of free cash flow, given working capital headwinds, adjusting for working capital, free cash flow was closer to $60 million. Inventory was up 2% from last year $3.1 billion. Rising inventory as sales are falling is generally a concern for me, as it increases the risk that KSS will need to resort to discounting to move merchandise. However, having a bit more inventory before tariffs take effect does help to delay that cost pressure. As such, I am comfortable with the company’s inventory position, though I would expect it to more sharply draw down in coming quarters.

Kohl Has Excessive Financial Leverage, And Debt Reduction Will Be Prolonged

Kohl’s does continue to have a challenged balance sheet with $2.1 billion of debt. That gives it 1.5x debt/EBITDA. However, it also has $5.3 billion of leases, leaving it with 4.7x leverage. This value of leases reflects what management expects to exercise, which is an average of 19 years. On a contractual basis, it has $2.5 billion of leases, averaging five years, for 2.9x leverage.

While management is highlighting the 2.9x level, I would note KSS would not have much of a business if it let all leases lapse in five years. As it is, the 4.7x only reflects extensions that are probable. While KSS could ultimately close stores more aggressively, I believe the 4.7x level is more indicative of its financial position than the 2.9x level. KSS remains an over-leveraged company that needs to focus on debt reduction.

Back in March, I expected Kohl’s to generate about $250 million of free cash flow this year, aided by working capital improvement, with underlying free cash flow closer to $150-175 million. This was based on my view that the company would deliver at the low end of guidance. With incremental tariffs and a slowing economy, I do see risk of guidance cuts in the future and see results coming at or below the low end.

With about $1.40 in underlying free cash flow, shares do have a free cash flow yield north of 15%, which is discounting meaningful long-term decline. However, after its dividend, KSS will retain just ~$170 million in free cash flow this year and closer to $100 million in future years. Given the need to reduce leverage by at least 1x, or over $1 billion, it will likely take 5+ years, if all goes well, for investors to see capital returns beyond its current 5.5% dividend yield.

Short Interest And Weak Financials Result In A "Hold" Rating

For a company suffering secular decline and with added cyclical pressures, this is simply not compelling. If investors want to own a retail turnaround, I would much rather be in Macy’s (M), which has seen more traction and has a far stronger balance sheet. As discussed in my last article on Macy's, that company has over $900 million of cash, allowing it to actively repurchase shares now, unlike Kohl's, which must focus on debt reduction. We have also seen Macy's more aggressively close stores, and its go-forward business saw a relatively narrow 0.9% decline last quarter, far better than Kohl's ~4% drop.

Now, one positive for KSS shares is how hated they already are. It has a remarkable 45% short interest. With sufficient liquidity and positive free cash flow, KSS does not face near-term bankruptcy risk.

In situations similar to this, we have seen short squeezes occur that can send shares surging, at least temporarily. Positioning here does appear very one-sided, especially given the pressure on Kohl’s is one of ongoing, long-term decline and not a near-term collapse. As a result, I do not see an urgency to sell shares, as a technical shift could cause a rally. But if we saw shares get past $12, I would begin selling.

However, whether a short squeeze does occur or when it may happen is unknowable. From a fundamental perspective, I do not see a compelling reason to add shares, given recent news and its balance sheet. As such, I remain a hold and suggest investors stay on the sideline, looking for better opportunities elsewhere.

data.title: Don't Take The June Jobs Report At Face Value
data.description:
Introduction

Regular readers of mine will know that I'm a big time macro data nerd, meaning I get up early on days like July 3rd to watch for the Bureau of Labor Statistics ("BLS") jobs reports, as it can tell us a lot about the current state of the US economy. While jobs data is a little backwards looking — the July release of numbers is for June — it is the most up-to-date employment report the BLS produces.

The headline figures on the report were very good on this report, with the U.S. adding 147,000 jobs in June, which brings the overall unemployment rate to 4.1%, down from 4.2%.

The headline unemployment rate ("U-3") has been steady for a while now, having risen above 4% about a year ago. It hasn't budged much since, only fluctuating around the 4% mark.

Regarding sector performance, the largest change was in state and local governments, which added over ten times as many jobs as were cut by the Federal government. While we saw a reduction of 7,000 jobs at the Federal level, total government employment rose 73,000 in June.

Notably, we also saw continued rises month-over-month in education, health, and hospitality. Manufacturing suffered another decline, losing 7,000 jobs.

The biggest, more bullish news for me from the report? The BLS revised the last two months of data up by 16,000 jobs. In the last few months, we've seen consistent downward revisions, meaning that we have been giving up much of the gains we make month to month by losing them retroactively. The opposite came true with this report, where April and May are now looking higher than we expected. This is very positive and breaks a worrying trend.

Deeper Look at Jobs Data

Inside the report, we find all kinds of interesting data that may give us pause, especially given that I am starting to see cracks in the longer-term employment data for "prime age" workers.

When looking at the breakdown of unemployment, it is common to see it broken down into the duration of unemployment, and the longest group (27 weeks or more) increased by 190,000 on the report, more than the jobs we added. This figure now accounts for nearly a quarter of unemployed people. One of the reasons this is dangerous is because if these folks give up on their search, they leave the workforce, and then aren't counted as "unemployed" as per the U-3 headline metric.

We can see this manifest in the data this month, with the labor force participation rate fluctuating and now on the decline. This offset the unemployment rate and brought the figure down from 4.2% to 4.1% not because we employed that many more people, but because they stopped looking for work altogether.

The BLS picks up on this further down in their report:

Among those not in the labor force who wanted a job, the number of people marginally attached to the labor force increased by 234,000 in June to 1.8 million. These individuals wanted and were available for work and had looked for a job sometime in the prior 12 months but had not looked for work in the 4 weeks preceding the survey. The number of discouraged workers, a subset of the marginally attached who believed that no jobs were available for them, increased by 256,000 in June to 637,000.

We also saw an increase in teen unemployment, up from 12% to 14% YoY, but teen unemployment typically doesn't affect the broader economy very much, at least not on its own.

We also saw the average workweek move down a bit, by 0.1 hours, which could be a sign that employers are reducing hours, one of the tactics they use to avoid layoffs. Of course, this data is also due to fluctuate, and the movement this month is within this margin. This is something to watch over time, as it is a leading indicator that layoffs are coming.

What Will the Fed Do?

All of this begs the question: does good headline jobs data reinforce the Fed's "wait and see" approach?

I think so. The Fed is keeping rates relatively high ("moderately restrictive" in Powell's words) as a dry powder to deal with tariff-induced inflation that may be coming now that we are getting closer and closer to the deadline for countries to make a deal with the U.S., and publicly announced progress on the UK, China, and Vietnam not being enough to persuade the Fed that it will all be alright.

They are trying to act with prudence; if they drop rates now while the jobs data is relatively positive, what will they do later if the jobs market rolls over? Once rates are sub-2%, it's very difficult to lower them further without creating bubble-like behavior in financial markets.

This data makes the odds of a cut later this month even less likely than it already was, and I really haven't expected anything until September at the earliest. This data is very unlikely to change minds at the Fed.

Conclusion

Ultimately, while I still see major cracks forming in parts of the market, the headline figures are still solid. Assuaging some concerns of mine, the report revised the past two months of data, bucking a trend of consistent downward revisions that had brought up some hesitation about the data in the past.

The June report held fairly strong even beneath the headlines, bar a few key data points that I continue to track and see as important for forward-looking takes on the U.S. economy, such as the amount of marginally attached workers, youth unemployment rates, and the reduction of weekly hours worked.

None of this raises alarm bells for me or the Fed, and they are likely to continue holding rates where they are, seeing the current data as justifying continued monetary policy restrictions.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Why I'm Buying Oscar's Panic Dip
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The Centene (CNC) shock has rattled the ACA sector, dragging Oscar Health (NYSE:OSCR) down by ~15-20% on fears of higher-than-expected claims risk. But this selloff looks like pure overreaction. Oscar just posted record Q1 results, 42% revenue growth, expanding membership to over 2 million, and a lean sub-16% SG&A ratio showing real operating leverage.

Its footprint and risk profile are different from Centene’s, so the morbidity spike isn’t a direct hit. Meanwhile, Oscar’s full-stack tech model and Campaign Builder AI keep driving high NPS and retention, fueling its unique flywheel for scalable growth.

Yes, policy shifts can add bumps, but Oscar’s structural cost advantage and $4.9 billion cash buffer mean it can ride out short-term noise. The ACA market’s core demand is intact, and Oscar’s undervalued multiple leaves plenty of upside. I see this panic dip as a buying window, that’s why I initiated a position during this dip.

Centene’s $1.8B ACA Shock: A Caution Flag, Not a Death Knell for Oscar

Centene announced on July 1 that preliminary 2025 ACA data revealed far greater morbidity in its exchange members than previously assumed. It reduced its risk-adjustment transfer estimate for the year by $1.8 billion (lowering 2025 EPS by approximately $2.75). Centene cited slower-than-projected market growth and a sicker-than-anticipated membership in its main states. This shook the market: Oscar shares declined ~15-20% with the announcement.

The Centene shock is a warning for Oscar but not a definitive hit. Oscar is also a part of the ACA risk pool, so higher-than-anticipated claims could do the same to its revenue transfer. But there is no indication from Oscar leadership that signaled any surprise on this front. In reality, Oscar provided healthy Q1’25 results (42% growth in revenues, record profit), indicating that any higher health risk is perhaps already incorporated into prices or balanced out by membership shifts.

Geographic mix does count: Centene's warning depended upon a subset of its footprint states (22/29 states that were analyzed), while the footprint for Oscar (18 states) consists of a different mix of states and socio-economic profile. However, the SEC filings by Oscar specifically caution that risk-adjustment variability “may impact our revenue… and have a material adverse effect on results” will have. Investors will be looking at the forthcoming results of Oscar (particularly any reference to risk-adjustment trends) to confirm.

Market Sentiment & Barclays Downgrade

In early July 2025, sentiment turned cautious on Wall Street. Barclays initiated coverage of Oscar at an Underweightrating as they argued the recent stock run-up was unsustainable against the backdrop of impending policy risk. The analyst note pointed out “asymmetric downside” should ACA subsidies disintegrate or CSR funding be cut.

Barclays assumed >100 bp of MLR pressure in 2026 (lowering EPS by ~30 ¢) and warned that Oscar's estimate of >$2.25 EPS in 2027 was aggressive (they predicted ~$1.28). Specifically, Barclays noted that 34% of Oscar members are in Bronze products (extremely subsidized with no-premium should CSR be continued), and such elimination could lead to outsized attrition and erode the risk pool.

Oscar’s plan to grow from 10 million lives in 2024 to 16 million by 2027 implies a ~19% CAGR over three years, aggressive but achievable given its recent double-digit growth in core ACA plans and expansion into Medicare Advantage and small group markets. The extra million lives from new markets and products shows Oscar isn’t just banking on organic churn; it’s actively pushing into underpenetrated states and adding new plan types to broaden its base.

Plus, the additional 4 million above that reflects deeper penetration in existing markets, which is realistic as scale efficiencies and brand recognition improve retention. If execution holds, and Oscar can keep its medical loss ratios in check, this bigger footprint could mean real leverage on fixed costs, closing the gap with larger incumbents faster than Barclays’ flat estimate implies.

This bearish thesis is at odds with prior momentum: the stock had surged more than 50% in June 2025 after a strong Q4 ’24 results and enthusiasm from investors. The Barclays downgrade and the news from Centene combined triggered a sell-off that took ~15-20% out of Oscar on July 2. Sectorally, the guidance retreat at Centene also took a toll across other insurers.

Overall, the market is now fearful of the ACA-specific headwind while near-term fundamentals at Oscar remain strong. The downgrade highlights how upside at Oscar is critically reliant upon policy developments.

Oscar Eyes 2 Million+ Members, Sub-16% SG&A for FY25 Growth Surge

Oscar Health has scalable growth and effective cost management in the individual insurance market. In Q1-FY25, Oscar had a record-low SG&A expense ratio of 15.8% with a big 2.6%-point improvement YoY. This improvement was based on a combination of fixed cost leverage (led 40% to the improvement), variable cost efficiencies (15%), and reductions in broker fees and taxes.

This trend points to considerable savings relative to the company’s $3 billion revenue in Q1-FY25 with a 42% YoY increase through a 41% surge in effectuated membership to 2 million. So, Oscar’s moves to scale operations and reduce per-member administrative costs is an edge towards growth in a market of thin margins and regulatory issues.

The company’s tech infra advancements like the live chat feature for Virtual Urgent Care have reduced member response times by 90% and boosted provider efficiency by 28%. This is a tangible improvement in service delivery. With that, an AI tool for care guides has accelerated the resolution of member inquiries. In short, these advancements boost member experience and reduce operational overhead, pushing Oscar to allocate resources toward member acquisition and product development.

For instance, the launch of Oscar Community Resources with Find Help (a social care network that is connecting members to services like housing and transportation) illustrates how tech can be used in medical care to address health determinants at scale with lowering costs (long-term) and improving clinical outcomes. These moves led to high digital engagement across its Individual and Family Plans (IFP) and Individual Coverage Health Reimbursement Arrangements (ICRA) membership.

Moreover, Oscar’s reaffirmed FY25 SG&A outlook of 17.6%–18.1% reflects some seasonality (expected). The Q1-FY25 SG&A ratio of 15.8% is considerably lower than historical levels pointing to a structural shift in cost control. If SG&A ratio continues to drop, Oscar’s operations are at a competitive advantage in the sector struggles with higher administrative expense ratios. $4.9 billion in cash with $1.5 billion in capital and surplus at its insurance subsidiaries enables Oscar to scale its platform without relying heavily on external financing. The scalability of Oscar’s tech derives the capability to handle rapid membership growth. The 41% YoY increase in effectuated members to 2 million in Q1-FY25 indicates the platform’s capacity to absorb growth without proportional increases in costs.

Further, this scalability is vital in the Affordable Care Act (ACA) market. Here, membership drives revenue and bottomline. By leveraging data analytics and AI, Oscar optimizes risk adjustment and care management like the condition-focused plans that targets specific health demand. These plans have performed strongly and led to retention and new member acquisition.

These plans rely on tech to personalize care, reducing churn that was minimal in Q1-FY25 (as per the management) despite regulatory shifts affecting enrollment. In Q1-FY25, there was big overlap with a competitor exiting the ACA exchanges in FY26. This is presenting an opportunity to capture market share through systematic pricing and superior member experience.

Overall, the ability to integrate new members through a lean cost structure, boosts Oscar’s stock price upside potential. OSCR has a forward P/S ratio of 0.46 (close against a sector median of 2.82 that reflects over 6x undervaluation. Similarly, price-to-cash-flow is 2.6x massively undervalued against the sector median of 14.72. So, there is a clear mispricing here.

Oscar Projects 10%+ H2 Membership Decline Amid Policy Shifts

Oscar Health’s core downside is its exposure to regulatory and enrollment policy changes that introduces big uncertainty into its membership and revenue projections. For effectuated members, management anticipates a decline to ~1.8 million by FY25-end due to the proposed termination of continuous Special Enrollment Periods (SEP) for individuals at or below 150% of the federal poverty level.

This policy shift is combined with potential expiration of enhanced premium tax credits and CMS’s program integrity initiatives (targeting fraud, waste, and abuse) can reduce industry-wide enrollment by 3%–9% as per CMS estimates. These changes directly impact Oscar’s topline largely based on membership growth.

Moreover, the medical loss ratio (MLR) in Q1-FY25 was 75.4% that is up 1.2%-points from 74.2% in Q1-FY24. This is partly due to a $31 million unfavorable prior-period development related to a $92 million increase in FY24 risk adjustment payables. This increase reflects the sensitivity of Oscar’s financials that are heavily influenced by regulatory policies.

For instance, the end of continuous SEP may alter the risk by reducing low-income enrollments, possibly increasing premiums. The combination of integrity regulations and subsidy changes can lead to a high single-digit impact on pricing for FY26. This is pushing premium trends above the Consumer Price Index (CPI). This is a departure from recent years themes where trends were at or below CPI. This shift can deter price-sensitive consumers that can reduce membership retention and growth.

Additionally, Oscar’s dependence on the ACA market boosts its vulnerability to policy shifts. The individual market is the core American healthcare with a record-low uninsured rate of 8%. But it faces risks of returning to double-digit uninsured levels if subsidies expire and enrollment windows shorten. Why? The shortened enrollment periods limit consumers’ ability to shop for plans mainly in FY26 when a competitor’s exit may force members to seek new coverage.

So, the regulatory issues complicates Oscar’s forecasting and pricing strategies as observed in the reaffirmation of FY25 revenue outlook ($11.2–$11.3 billion) despite exceeding Q1 membership expectations. In short, the anticipated membership decline in H2-FY25 points to the issues in maintaining growth momentum amid policy volatility.

While Oscar’s tech strategy can counter these operational impacts through efficient member onboarding. The company’s strong retention and new membership growth in Q1-FY25 reflect the value of its plan designs. So, Oscar’s strength is already tested by macro factors beyond its control. For instance, the $31 million unfavorable prior-period development in Q1-FY25 points how risk adjustment true-ups can erode bottomline when regulatory shifts alter risk pool composition. The company’s proactive engagement with policymakers to advocate for extended enrollment periods and stable subsidies is a main factor to monitor.

Takeaway

Oscar’s selloff looks like market fear, not a thesis break. Its tech-driven cost edge, record membership growth, and strong cash buffer give it resilience. Short-term ACA noise won’t derail its flywheel. I’m positioned now, expecting the market to reprice this undervalued, scalable disruptor.

data.title: NYLI Epoch Global Equity Yield Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

  • Global equity markets were volatile again during the first quarter of the year, ending the period with mixed returns. Value indices were positive and outperformed growth indices which logged negative returns as the Magnificent 7* collectively fell into bear market territory. Most sectors were positive overall with energy, utilities, and financials generating the strongest returns. Consumer discretionary, communication services, and information technology were the sectors in negative territory.
  • After a strong start to the year with some indices hitting record highs in mid-late February, stocks ended the quarter on a weak note driven in large part by growth fears that followed uncertainty around tariffs and the broader Trump 2.0 policy agenda. Tariffs were one of the biggest stories in the quarter as ramped up tariff threats in March, declaration of "Liberation Day" and an announcement of 25% tariffs on auto imports all weighed on investor sentiment. Softer data, worries over disinflation traction, and cracks in the Artificial Intelligence (AI) secular growth narrative were also among the catalysts that contributed to the selloff in the back half of the quarter. The quarter also featured some increasingly hawkish fedspeak, although the Fed's latest Summary of Economic Projections continues to show expectations for two rate cuts this year. As expected, the Fed kept interest rates on hold at 4.25-4.50% during the quarter. The European Central Bank (ECB) cut interest rates by 25 basis points in both January and March decisively tilting monetary policy toward supporting growth in Q1, countering a sluggish Eurozone economy and the fallout from U.S. trade tensions.

FUND INFORMATION (As of 03/31/25)

Fund Inception

12/27/05

Total Net Assets (all classes)

$741.5 M

Distribution Frequency

Quarterly

Number of Holdings

Annual Turnover Rate2 (%)

FUND EXPENSES (%)

1.14

I

0.89

0.84

4 No initial sales charge applies on investments of $1 million or more (and certain other qualified purchases). However, a contingent deferred sales charge of 1.00% may be imposed on certain redemptions made within 18 months of the date of purchase on shares that were purchased without an initial sales charge.

Portfolio Highlights

  • For the quarter, the NYLI Epoch Global Equity Yield Fund (the “Fund”) posted a return of 3.6% while the broad market returned negative 1.8% as measured by the MSCI World Index. It was a volatile quarter that put the benefits of the strategy's defensive attributes on full display, leading the benchmark through a choppy grind upwards for the first half of the quarter and then providing limited downside participation when tariff-panic drove a precipitous decline in the back half.
  • Absolute return was positive in almost all sectors, with the largest contributions coming from health care and consumer staples. Health care return was driven primarily by strength in pharmaceutical holdings. Tobacco stocks were the largest tailwind to return in consumer staples. Both sectors benefitted from a rotation to more defensive market segments in the face of rising volatility.
  • Relative return was very strong for the quarter, with the strategy leading the benchmark by a wide margin, posting positive results in a quarter where the index declined. The strategy finished behind the MSCI World High Dividend Yield Index. Health care provided the largest contribution to relative return on the back of stock selection as a mix of pharmaceutical and health care provider and services companies performed well in the quarter. Communication services contributed next most, owing to stock selection. Exposure to select diversified telecommunications holdings drove performance in the sector, as the industry benefitted from the quarter's defensive rotation. Material contributions also came from stock selection and underweight allocations to the information technology and consumer discretionary sectors.

What Helped:

Among the largest individual contributors to return were Philip Morris (PM) and IBM (IBM). Philip Morris is a U.S.-domiciled international tobacco company and the manufacturer of the largest international brand – Marlboro. Shares outperformed over the quarter which reflected a positive reaction to the company's fourth quarter results and commentary at subsequent industry conferences. More recently, investors have reacted favorably to weakness in the U.S. dollar (PM reports in USD but earns >85% of revenue in other currencies) as well as its profile as a safe haven in the face of macroeconomic concerns.

Longer-term, Philip Morris consistently generates attractive levels of free cash flow and returns cash to shareholders through a significant and growing dividend. IBM is a leading provider of software solutions, consulting, application management, servers, and storage systems. Shares outperformed on strong consulting bookings and growth in its AI consulting business. The company's backlog has shown sustained increases supporting mid-single digits growth for 2025. Investment in AI, multi-cloud solutions, and a mainframe cycle refresh in 2025 should support growth even if traditional consulting remains pressured as its contribution to overall results shrinks. The company pays a well-covered, growing dividend.

What Hurt:

Among the largest detractors were Broadcom (AVGO) and Hewlett Packard (HPE). Broadcom is a designer and manufacturer of digital and analog semiconductors focused on connectivity. It also develops and maintains software for mainframe applications. Shares underperformed as investors pulled back from AI themed names on worries of a capex slowdown. Early indications are spending on AI will continue to expand in 2026 despite DeepSeek claims of similar results at a lower cost. Revenues have yet to ramp-up and will need to for long term investment but near term the promise of AI is large, both in the transformation of industries and potential productivity gains. Being late or not committing to AI could have drastic consequence which should keep investment elevated over the near term. Broadcom returns cash to shareholders via an attractive dividend with a target of paying out 50% of free cash flow. The balance of cash generation is used to fund debt reduction, share repurchases, and/or accretive M&A. Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) is an IT solutions provider of compute, intelligent edge, hybrid cloud (storage), high performance compute & artificial intelligence, and financial services which supports its enterprise focused customers. Shares underperformed on poor earnings results where server margin guidance was reset lower. It will take a while to clear this inventory. particularly on older NVIDIA Hopper based AI servers.

We do not believe that the current pressure is due to a slowdown in overall capital spend on AI, rather it is a result of regrettable inventory and pricing management. Over the medium term we believe that being late or not committing to AI could have drastic consequence which should keep spending on AI servers elevated over the near term. Hewlett Packard Enterprises returns cash through a combination of a growing dividend and share repurchases.

*The Magnificent 7 stocks are a group of mega-cap stocks that drive the market’s performance due to their heavy weighting in major stock indexes such as the Standard & Poor’s 500 and the Nasdaq 100. It includes Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), Apple (AAPL), Meta Platforms (META), Microsoft (MSFT), NVIDIA (NVDA), and Tesla (TSLA).

Outlook and Positioning

  • While the U.S. economy has remained fundamentally strong, stickier than expected inflation alongside turbulence derived from rapid tariff announcements out of the White House have called into question soft-landing expectations. We expect some degree of inflationary pressures, demand erosion, impediments to economic growth and continued volatility as impacts resulting from the recently announced tariffs and a possible prolonged trade war. The ultimate impact on the real economy and corporate fundamentals is largely dependent on the duration and magnitude of tariffs globally. The Fed is in easing mode, but the path forward for monetary policy has been clouded by growth pressures and upside risks to inflation stemming from tariffs. Going forward, supportive policy for the labor market will need to be carefully balanced against the risk of reigniting inflation. Markets' initial exuberance over Donald Trump's victory has reversed, as the aggressive implementation of broad- based tariffs has eroded risk appetites recently. The administration has chosen to lean on tariffs as bargaining chips in geopolitical negotiations, making it difficult to discern what policy proposals are genuine versus what is being used to posture for negotiating power. This uncertainty has driven skittishness in equities and clouded capital market forecasts.
  • Outside the U.S., the geopolitical and macroeconomic backdrop remains more challenging. A general uptick in geopolitical tension has remained an ongoing trend, with broad deglobalization and realignment of global supply chains via onshoring/reshoring and announced tariffs threatening to suppress long term growth potential.
  • We are cautiously optimistic on the path forward for the economy and for equities from a fundamental standpoint, but recent developments place more emphasis on "caution" than "optimism". With the way forward so clouded by the current trade climate, the wide array of possible outcomes is something that investors should be considering while managing risk exposures and positioning for the year ahead. We believe shareholder yield is poised to capture the productivity of a growing economy while remaining defensively positioned for resiliency should growth begin to falter. The Fund offers a diversified portfolio of high-quality companies with reliable free cash flow growth and long-standing track records for sound capital allocation practices, providing durable stability and long-term consistency. History has shown that remaining disciplined has rewarded patient investors over time. No one can predict how or when the current tariff war will be resolved. But we do have a firm conviction that the types of companies we invest in should have the ability to adapt and endure through this environment and even emerge stronger on the other side. At the portfolio level, diversification across regions, sectors, and business models provides an additional cushion against today's uncertainty and volatility.

The opinions expressed are those of Epoch Investment Partners, Inc. as of the date of this report and are subject to change. There is no guarantee that any forecast made will come to pass. This material does not constitute investment advice and is not intended as an endorsement of any specific investment.

The Morningstar Medalist RatingTM is the summary expression of Morningstar’s forward-looking analysis of investment strategies as offered via specific vehicles using a rating scale of Gold, Silver, Bronze, Neutral, and Negative. The Medalist Ratings indicate which investments Morningstar believes are likely to outperform a relevant index or peer group average on a risk-adjusted basis over time. Investment products are evaluated on three key pillars (People, Parent, and Process) which, when coupled with a fee assessment, forms the basis for Morningstar’s conviction in those products’ investment merits and determines the Medalist Rating they’re assigned. Pillar ratings take the form of Low, Below Average, Average, Above Average, and High. Pillars may be evaluated via an analyst’s qualitative assessment (either directly to a vehicle the analyst covers or indirectly when the pillar ratings of a covered vehicle are mapped to a related uncovered vehicle) or using algorithmic techniques. Vehicles are sorted by their expected performance into rating groups defined by their Morningstar Category and their active or passive status. When analysts directly cover a vehicle, they assign the three pillar ratings based on their qualitative assessment, subject to the oversight of the Analyst Rating Committee, and monitor and reevaluate them at least every 14 months. When the vehicles are covered either indirectly by analysts or by algorithm, the ratings are assigned monthly. For more detailed information about these ratings, including their methodology, please go to global.morningstar.com/managerdisclosures/.

The Morningstar Medalist Ratings are not statements of fact, nor are they credit or risk ratings. The Morningstar Medalist Rating (i) should not be used as the sole basis in evaluating an investment product, (ii) involves unknown risks and uncertainties which may cause expectations not to occur or to differ significantly from what was expected, (iii) are not guaranteed to be based on complete or accurate assumptions or models when determined algorithmically, (iv) involve the risk that the return target will not be met due to such things as unforeseen changes in changes in management, technology, economic development, interest rate development, operating and/or material costs, competitive pressure, supervisory law, exchange rate, tax rates, exchange rate changes, and/or changes in political and social conditions, and (v) should not be considered an offer or solicitation to buy or sell the investment product. A change in the fundamental factors underlying the Morningstar Medalist Rating can mean that the rating is subsequently no longer accurate.

Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks. These risks may be greater for emerging markets. Certain environmental, social, and governance ("ESG") criteria may be considered when evaluating an investment opportunity. This may result in the Fund having exposure to securities or sectors that are significantly different than the composition of the Fund's benchmark and performing differently than other funds and strategies in its peer group that do not take into account ESG criteria.
